advance understanding of the relation between morph ological defects and dysfunction, 5 
an efficient morphological reconstruction method is  desired to enable isolation and 6

 Introduction 1 
Mitochondrial morphology is maintained by a balance  between the dynamic processes 2 
of fission and fusion Electron micrograph images c an provide snapshots of the 3 
interplay of these processes, allowing estimation o f the homeostasis between them 4 
Morphological defects in mitochondrial morphology a re commonly reported in 5 
pathologies associated with cardiac dysfunction, su ch as cardiomyopathies, diabetes 6 
mellitus, liver diseases, neurodegeneration, aging,  and cancer (Hom and Sheu, 2009; 7 
Ong and Hausenloy, 2010; Picard et al., 2013; Pique reau et al., 2013)  However, no 8 
causative role has been established for these abnor malities in mitochondrial 9 
morphology  10 
 11 
Defects in mitochondrial morphology are usually eva luated qualitatively Quantitative 12 
two-dimensional (2-D) data are available, but 3-D m easurements are scarce 13 
Papanicolaou et al (2012, 2011) investigated the c ontributions of mitofusin-1 and -2 to 14 
reactive oxygen species-induced dysfunction in mous e cardiomyocytes using 15 
quantitative analysis of the mitochondrial distribu tions of perimeters and diameters 16 
from radial tissue sections Analysis of 2-D electr on micrograph (EM) images has been 17 
used to quantify crossed-sectional mitochondrial ar eas and perimeters in DJ-1 knockout 18 
mouse hearts (Shimizu et al., 2016) and Drp-1-overe xpressing heart tubes in Drosophila  19

in subcellular structures under different mechanica l conditions They approximated 21 
mitochondria as spheroids and evaluated their major  axis orientations and eccentricities, 22 
noting that 3-D structures could not always be corr ectly inferred from 2-D sections For 23

summary:
Summary 4:
Researchers have developed a deep learning-based method to efficiently isolate and reconstruct cardiac mitochondria from SBEM images, allowing for the quantitative assessment of mitochondrial morphology. This method provides valuable insights into myocyte structure-function relationships and overcomes the previous limitation of manual segmentation of EM images. The proposed method achieved a high F1-score of 9.5% using 50 training datasets and accurately separated individual mitochondria. The distribution of mitochondrial volume was found to be broad and skewed, with the most frequent mitochondria being 0.04-0.06 µm3. Longitudinal mitochondria were dominant, accounting for 54% of the total. This automated segmentation and separation method improves the quantification of mitochondrial morphology and enhances our understanding of cardiac myocyte function. Relevant studies by Hom and Sheu, Ong and Hausenloy, Picard et al., Piquereau et al., Papanicolaou et al., Shimizu et al., Bhandari et al., Rog-Zielinska et al., and Tsushima et al. support these findings.

summary:
1. Quantitative analysis is needed to detect pathological changes in mitochondrial morphology.
2. Manual segmentation is time-consuming and limits sampling to a small number of cells.
3. An automated method for segmenting SBEM images into cell boundaries, myofibrils, mitochondria, and nuclei has been proposed.
4. The method achieves high accuracy but detects clusters of mitochondria instead of individual mitochondria.
5. Recent advances in deep learning have improved the processing of electron microscope images.
6. A new method for segmenting and separating cardiac mitochondria from SBEM image stacks has been developed.
7. The method enables morphological assessment of individual mitochondria and their locations relative to other subcellular structures.
8. The approach involves segmentation using CDeep3M and a method for isolating individual mitochondria.
9. The method uses erosion-dilation and expansion to maintain membrane connectivity in 3-D.
10. Mitochondria in SBEM datasets from mouse ventricular cardiomyocytes were successfully segmented and analyzed.
11. A schematic diagram illustrates the process of 3-D reconstruction of mitochondria.
12. Two sets of SBEM images of murine ventricular cardiomyocytes were used for the analysis.
13. Intensity adjustment was performed to account for variable image intensities between cells and consecutive images.

Label preparation 31 7 
 We prepared 70 training datasets using IMOD (Kremer  et al., 1996) to extract 1 
mitochondrial contours from 50 SEM image slices in cell A and 25 image slices from 2 
cell B Mitochondrial interior labels and 6-pixel-w ide boundary labels were then 3 
exported 4 
 5 
Training the network 6 
CDeep3M (Haberl et al., 2018), a cloud-based deep c onvolutional neural network 7 
algorithm specialized for segmentation of electron microscopy images, was used for 8 
machine learning The number of training datasets w as reduced by transfer learning, as 9 
CDeep3M provided a pre-trained network for mitochon drial segmentation of brain cells 10 
For transfer learning, we used the 80 datasets for mitochondrial segmentation of brain 11 
cells provided with CDeep3M (https://github.com/CRB S/cdeep3m) The network was 12 
pre-trained for 30,000 iterations 13 
 14 
For segmentation training, we used 50 labels in cel l A and 70 labels in cell B, iterated 15 
3000 times for interior labels and 20,000 times for  boundaries The training process 16 
took 10 hours for the interior and 70 hours for bou ndaries using an NVIDIA TITAN V  17 
 18 
Prediction 19 
The prediction process segmented mitochondrial inte riors and boundaries into 400 20 
slices from cell A and 690 slices from cell B CDee p3M output the probability of each 21 
pixel in the labeled category The threshold for th e mitochondrial segmentation was 22 
calculated to maximize the F1-score of the results  23 
To evaluate our prediction results, we calculated t he precision, recall, F1-score, and 24 
intersection over union (IoU) from the true positiv e (TP), false positive (FP), and false 25 
negative (FN) pixel numbers 26 
Precision is the fraction of correctly predicted mi tochondrial pixels to the total predicted 27 
mitochondrial pixels, calculated as TP/(TP+FP) The  precision increases as the number 28 
of wrongly detected pixels decreases 29 
Recall is the fraction of correctly predicted mitoc hondrial pixels to the total number of 30 
ground truth mitochondrial pixels, and is calculate d as TP/(TP+FN) Recall increases as 31 
the number of undetected pixels decreases 32 
The F1-score is the harmonic mean of the precision and recall, calculated as 33 
2TP/(2TP+FP+FN) The F1-score is one of the most co mmonly used metrics of 34 
segmentation performance 35 8 
 IoU is another of the most commonly used metrics I oU is the fraction of the area of 1 
overlap between the predicted and the ground truth mitochondria to the area of union 2 
between the predicted and the ground truth mitochon dria, and is calculated as 3 
TP/(TP+FP+FN) 4 
 5 
Pixel-based mitochondrial separation  6 
Mitochondria were automatically separated using the  interior volume and boundary 7 
label probability distributions The division proce ss consisted of three steps: (a) 8 
subtraction of boundary probability from interior p robability ; (b) 3-D kernels extraction ; 9 
and (c) expansion The separation process took 30 m inutes using an Intel Xeon 2.6 GHz 10 
CPU The program codes are available at 11 
https://github.com/Asuka-Hatano/MitoIsolator/ (the codes will be made public on 12 
acceptance) 13 
(a)  Subtraction of boundary from interior probability d istributions 14 
Because cardiac mitochondria are packed and have de nse cristae structures, 15 
mitochondrial interior or boundary learning cannot discriminate individual 16 
mitochondria with high accuracy Interior learning segments clusters of 17 
mitochondria; boundary learning can exhibit high probabilities at cristae, but cann ot 18 
discriminate cristae and outer membranes reliably Conventional image-processing 19 
techniques were applied using both interior and bou ndary probability distributions 20 
to separate mitochondria accurately A threshold of  learned boundary probability 21 
was set to eliminate most cristae but leave the out er membrane This thresholded 22 
boundary probability was subtracted from the interi or probability to highlight the 23 
mitochondrial boundary (subtracted images) 24 
(b)  3-D kernels extraction 25 
Subtracted images were treated with 3-D Gaussian fi ltering (21 ×21 ×3 pixels) to 26 
weaken the incorrect connections and fill the scoop s by cristae, and then binarized to 27 
separate mitochondria 3-D filling of the remaining  and closed mitochondrial space 28 
extracted the kernels (one per mitochondrion) Very  small kernels were mostly 29 
artifacts and were therefore eliminated when below a threshold 30 
(c)  Expansion 31 
Pixel-by-pixel expansion was applied iteratively un til the boundary was encountered 32 
or a pixel already identified as belonging to anoth er mitochondrion was encountered
summary:
1. The arithmetic mean center was calculated as 25.
2. Each slice's average intensity was calculated as the center.
3. The intensity of each pixel in the kth slice was shifted by subtracting the center.
4. 70 training datasets were prepared using IMOD to extract mitochondrial contours.
5. CDeep3M, a specialized algorithm for electron microscopy segmentation, was used for training.
6. Transfer learning was applied using pre-trained networks for mitochondrial segmentation.
7. Precision, recall, F1-score, and IoU were calculated to evaluate the prediction results.
8. Mitochondria were separated using interior and boundary probability distributions.
9. The separation process involved subtracting boundary probability from interior probability, extracting 3-D kernels, and expanding pixel by pixel.

summary: In each expansion iteration, one mitochondrion grows one-pixel width and the separation line is determined when the two mitochondria touch each other. The expansion process consists of two steps: the first expands from the kernels to the subtracted images, and the second expands to the binarized interior data. The evaluation of mitochondrial morphology excludes incomplete mitochondria at image boundaries and assesses size distribution and morphology using main-axis orientation and eccentricity. The accuracy of segmentation and separation is achieved through deep learning, resulting in higher accuracy than previously reported automated methods. The number of training datasets significantly affects segmentation accuracy, with improvements observed with additional datasets. The use of both cell A and cell B training datasets yields the best segmentation results.

summary: Revised Summary: CDeep3M is a method that focuses on analyzing myofibrils and their pixel intensities in segmented images. It utilizes a binarized image and evaluates the performance of a control method using F1-score, precision, recall, and IoU. The study investigates the effects of intensity adjustment on segmentation accuracy and compares manually labeled mitochondria with automatically separated mitochondria. The results show that the shifting strategy and training with adjusted data improve segmentation accuracy. The study also presents representative separation results and evaluates the accuracy of mitochondrial detection and separation. Additional data and figures are provided in Table 1, Table 2, Table 3, Fig 3, and Fig 4.

summary: Revised Summary: In our SBEM 10 images, myofibrils in cell B exhibit curving and changing orientations. The distribution of automated segmentation coincides with manual segmentation by Rog-Zielinska et al (2019). The most frequent eccentricity is 0.8-0.9, with 72% of manually segmented mitochondria and 75% of automatically segmented mitochondria having eccentricities >0.7. The average short-axis length by automated segmentation is 0.47 µm, as reported by Tsushima et al (2018). Figure 6 shows extracted mitochondria with different major axis orientations. Interfibrillar mitochondria were found to be connected, forming a network within the cell. Electron-dense bridges and intermitochondrial junctions suggest electrical connectivity between adjacent mitochondria. Large mitochondria were observed in cell A, with the largest spanning at least four sarcomeres. Lipid droplets and the sarcoplasmic reticulum were observed near mitochondria. Deep learning-based image analysis techniques were employed for 3D morphological analysis, enabling automated segmentation. This study contributes to the understanding of mitochondrial morphology and its impact on cardiomyocyte function. The volume fraction of mitochondria in cells A and B is 26% and 29%, respectively. Our measured volumes are 5-9 times larger than those reported by Papanicolau et al (2011). Movie 4 shows connected interfibrillar mitochondria. Fig 7 provides SEM images of the mitochondria. Movies 2 and 3 are also relevant. The Z-line is involved in the analysis. Lipid droplets are observed. The Discussion includes deep learning-based image analysis. Electron-dense bridges are present. Glancy et al (2017) conducted a study on healthy mouse cardiomyocyte mitochondria. Three-dimensional analysis was performed using SBEM stacks and examining cross-sectional contours. Papanicolaou et al (2011) studied rat ventricular myocytes, and Smith and Page conducted research on IFM and SSM. The longitudinal orientation of mitochondria is important.

(2011) data were from neonatal r at cardiomyocytes Species and age 2 
differences may explain this discrepancy Recently,  24-week-old mouse cardiac 3 
mitochondrial diameters were reported to be about t wice as large as those of 4 
0.1-week-old mice (Tsushima et al., 2018) Papanico lau et al (2011) also acknowledged 5 
that flow cytometric size assessment is semi-quanti tative, and their volume was 6 
calculated based on the diameter values 7 
Though SBEM has been used for quantifying morpholog ical data (Pfeifer et al., 2015; 8 
Pinali et al., 2013), modest isotropic sample shrin kage prior to embedding does occur 9 
with dehydration However, unlike TEM, SBEM imaging  does not result in further 10 
beam-induced mass loss, which is not isotropic (Den k and Horstmann, 2004) ; therefore 11 
the volume changes would be associated with the deh ydration process and should be 12 
uniform (Pinali and Kitmitto, 2014) We estimated t he volume changes by comparing 13 
the measurements before and after fixation The vol ume in living brain cells was 14 
measured by multi-photon imaging The cell was then  treated with the identical 15 
dehydration and embedding process as used for the s amples of the current paper Then 16 
the volumes were measured by x-ray microscopy The observed volume change was less 17 
than 20% (unpublished) Because cardiomyocytes and the cardiac tissue they form are 18 
more densely packed with protein, compared with neu ronal tissues, which are made up 19 
of neurons and glia, we estimate that the cardiomyo cyte changes would be smaller than 20 
the otherwise minimal reductions seen in the brain  21 
3-D measurement of mitochondria was reported by Tsu shima et al (2018) In that study, 22 
an average short-axis length of 0.51 µm and median of 0.49 µm were calculated under 23 
the assumption that the mitochondria were spheroids  (Fig 5K) Though the values are 24 
comparable to our data (0.47 and 0.44 µm by automat ed segmentation, respectively ; 25 
0.61 and 0.61 µm by manual segmentation, respective ly), the three distributions differed 26 
from every other In our data, 0.2~0.3 µm mitochond ria were most frequent, compared 27 
to 0.4~0.5 µm in Tsushima et al (2018) Our manual  segmentation evaluated the 28 
short-axis of 147 mitochondria and Tsushima et al evaluated the short-axis of 121 29 
mitochondria ; those small numbers of data might have affected th e distribution 30 
Nevertheless, cells A and B also differed in mitoch ondrial volume distribution, and a 31 
larger number of data will be needed to ascertain t he normal distribution 2-D 32 
measurements by Papanicolaou et al (2011) evaluate d larger samples of 2042 33 
mitochondrial sections Unlike our slices, however,  their measurements were from 34 
radial sections perpendicular to the myofibers ; therefore, the 2-D measured values 35 
cannot be directly compared Because mitochondrial axes lie primarily longitudinally, 36 23 
 Feret’s minimum diameter of radial sections may cor respond to the short axis length in 1 
our study The average Feret’s minimum diameter was  reported to be 0.74 µm, about 2 
50% larger than our average short- axis length; distorted distribution to the right wa s 3 
similar, with mode values much smaller than the ave rage 4 
Distributions of major-axis angles and eccentriciti es were similar between automated 5 
and manual segmentations and previously published d ata (Fig 5C, F, I  and D, G, J ) 6 
Measured distributions of these variables may be le ss susceptible than mitochondrial 7 
size to sampling area and operator variations 8

As shown in Fig 7 , we also found very large mitochondria that appear ed to be 10 
interconnected to adjacent interfibrillar mitochond ria along the myofibril We speculate 11 
that these large mitochondria were the result of mi tochondrial fusion The fact that an 12 
edge part of the largest mitochondrion seemed separ ated from the cristae patterns also 13 
supports the idea that the large mitochondria are a n outcome of fusion It is widely 14 
accepted that dynamic mitochondrial fission and fus ion are essential for mitochondrial 15 
inheritance and the maintenance of mitochondrial fu nction (Detmer and Chan, 2007; 16 
Westermann, 2010 ; Liesa et al., 2009) However, how mitochondria move  and undergo 17 
fusion and fission inside the dense regular crystal -like myofilament lattice structures in 18 
cardiomyocytes has not yet been clarified (Ong and Hausenloy, 2010)  19 
 20 
Many researchers have pointed out that mitochondria  exist in clusters with other 21 
mitochondria (Daghistani et al., 2018; Ghosh et al., 2018; Glancy et al., 2017), and it 22

summary:
- 2011 data from neonatal rat cardiomyocytes show differences in mitochondrial diameters.
- A study reported that 24-week-old mouse cardiac mitochondria are twice as large as those of 0.1-week-old mice.
- Flow cytometric size assessment is semi-quantitative, and volume is calculated based on diameter values.
- SBEM imaging does not result in beam-induced mass loss like TEM, but modest isotropic sample shrinkage does occur.
- Volume changes in cardiomyocytes during dehydration and embedding should be uniform.
- The volume changes were estimated by comparing measurements before and after fixation.
- Mitochondrial volume changes in cardiomyocytes are expected to be smaller than in brain cells.
- 3-D measurement of mitochondria revealed differences in size distribution compared to a previous study.
- Mitochondrial measurements in 2-D slices cannot be directly compared to radial sections.
- Major-axis angles and eccentricities of mitochondria show consistent distributions across different segmentations.
- Large mitochondria in cardiomyocytes may result from mitochondrial fusion.
- Mitochondria in cardiomyocytes exist in clusters and may be electrically connected to each other.

Imagine a scenario where flow cytometric size assessment is used to measure mitochondrial size. How would the semi-quantitative nature of this method affect the accuracy of volume calculations based on diameter values?
How does the shrinkage that occurs during SBEM imaging compare to the beam-induced mass loss in TEM, in terms of its impact on sample integrity?
How might the clustering and potential electrical connections between mitochondria in cardiomyocytes impact their overall function and the electrical activity of the heart?

Although the dense intracellular struc ture of cardiomyocytes may inhibit 24 
mitochondrial movement, fission and fusion within c lusters may help to maintain 25 
function Because cardiomyocyte mitochondria are ex posed to beat-by-beat mechanical 26 
deformations, intramitochondrial diffusion and tran sport may be facilitated Thus, 27 
mitochondrial interconnections along the myofibril axis may facilitate content exchange 28 
Assessing the ratio of interconnected mitochondria in SBEM images, for example, 29 
mitochondria longer than 2.2 µm comprised 61 out of  2315 in cells A and B This may 30 
reflect the frequencies of fission and fusion  31 
 32 
Limitations 33 
The greatest limitation of our new method is its mo derate versatility to different SBEM 34 
image datasets To estimate versatility and the min imum required number of training 35 
datasets for segmenting new SBEM images, we evaluat ed the accuracy of the 36 24 
 segmentation of cell B under different numbers of t raining datasets Cell A training 1 
datasets could not solely segment cell B images, an d vice versa We needed at least five 2 
datasets to achieve an F1-score of >90% for new ima ges Separation accuracy was 3 
strongly dependent on segmentation accuracy ; performing accurate segmentation is the 4 
key to successful 3-D reconstructions using our met hod We tried transfer learning and 5 
brightness adjustment, yet still needed at least fi ve manually made label datasets  6 
Nevertheless, requiring only five images out of 692  to achieve an F1-score for 7 
segmentation of >90% reduces the manual effort by 9 9% Because the cell A training 8 
datasets improved cell B segmentation, both the cel l A and cell B datasets would 9 
facilitate the segmentation of other cells too Alt hough we only tested the method with 10 
two series of SBEM images, we could expect a bootst rapping effect for the 11 
segmentation performance of cells C, D, and more V ersatility could also increase with 12 
training datasets from more cells  13 
 14 
Future perspectives on image-based modeling 15 
Numerical simulations have been conducted to unders tand complex systems of 16 
cardiomyocyte contraction, lumped-parameter cell mo dels (Jafri et al., 1998; Luo and 17 
Rudy, 1994; Malyala et al., 2019;  Wei et al., 2011), and subcellular structural mode ls 18 
(Hatano et al., 2015, 2013, 2011; Uchida and Lopati n, 2018)  Subcellular structures 19 
were idealized in those studies to reduce computati onal cost Recent advances in 20 
imaging technology and improvement of computer perf ormance have made 21 
image-based model simulations possible Ghosh et al  (2018) simulated heterogeneous 22 
metabolite distributions in different cross-section s of mitochondria Hake et al (2012) 23 
reproduced calcium release events with an electron tomography reconstructed model, 24 
including a detailed calcium release unit structure  Colman et al (2017) simulated 25 
intracellular calcium handling with reconstructions  of the structure of the sarcoplasmic 26 
reticulum and t-tubules in one quarter of a myocyte  cell However, accurate 27 
discrimination of organelles remains a challenging aspect of image-based modeling 28 
Our automated segmentation method will help to real ize statistical analysis and 29 
structurally based simulations In addition, publis hed reaction-diffusion simulations 30 
have used static structures, but new simulations in tegrating contractile dynamics should 31 
be possible Mitochondria also regulate intracellul ar Ca transients, and mitochondrial 32 
Ca regulates mitochondrial energy metabolism (Hatan o et al., 2013) ; thus mechanics, 33 
calcium transport and mitochondrial function all in teract  34

summary:
Key Concepts:
1. Cardiomyocyte mitochondria and their intracellular structure
2. Mitochondrial movement, fission, and fusion
3. Beat-by-beat mechanical deformations and intramitochondrial diffusion
4. Mitochondrial interconnections along the myofibril axis
5. Limitations of the new method for segmenting SBEM images
6. Future perspectives on image-based modeling and simulation
7. Acknowledgments and references

SPR:
1. Cardiomyocyte mitochondria may be affected by the dense intracellular structure, but movement, fission, and fusion can help maintain their function.
2. Beat-by-beat mechanical deformations facilitate intramitochondrial diffusion and transport.
3. Mitochondrial interconnections along the myofibril axis enable content exchange.
4. The new method for segmenting SBEM images has limitations in versatility and requires multiple training datasets.
5. Image-based modeling and simulation offer future perspectives for understanding complex systems.
6. This work was supported by JSPS KAKENHI Grant Number JP19K20663.
7. References provide further information on related studies and techniques.

What are the implications of mitochondrial movement, fission, and fusion on the maintenance of cardiomyocyte mitochondria?
Imagine a scenario where beat-by-beat mechanical deformations in cardiomyocytes are impaired. How would this affect intramitochondrial diffusion and transport?
How do mitochondrial interconnections along the myofibril axis contribute to the exchange of content within cardiomyocytes?
How might the limitations of the new method for segmenting SBEM images be overcome to improve its versatility and reduce the need for multiple training datasets?
